guest_role: Member of the global investment team at MIT Investment Management Company (MITIMCo)
assertiveness_why: Harry repeatedly plants his own data/thesis (e.g. '43% lower entry price', 'LPs do not chastise venture investors more') and pushes Ryan to defend or react, going beyond passive reception.
question_style: Mostly open, well-prepared multi-part questions, but Harry frequently interjects his own opinions or stats mid-question and asks Ryan to agree or push back, and he occasionally interrupts to sharpen a point rather than just receive the answer.
tone: Warm, matey and clearly reverent of the guest ('tables are turning', 'I love, love that you're here'), high energy, some self-deprecating asides ('crap question, forgive me'), overall fandom without being sycophantic on substance.
depth: Goes technical on LP mechanics: fund position sizing, drawdown beta, temporal diversification, secondary market discounts, structured-note downside protection, and direct co-investment underwriting; tracks specific deals (Coupang, Rippling, OpenAI) in detail.
harry_quotes: Is it harder than ever before today?
Do you agree with me or do you think we do get back
Are the incentive structures financially right in endowments?
Do you worry that they won't come back to you if you don't do them?
learnings: LPs increasingly evaluate managers on a see-pick-win-service framework, with winning allocation and founder servicing mattering most once a firm clears a baseline deal-flow/picking bar.
Early conviction in an unproven manager (Greenoaks) can hinge on qualitative signal from founder reference calls rather than any track record.
Compressed capital-raising and deployment cycles during 2021 broadly undermined temporal diversification, and most LPs were complicit rather than disciplined.
Direct co-investments alongside trusted GPs, especially with protective structures like senior secured notes, can offer better risk-adjusted conviction than blind-pool fund commitments.
Traditional endowment/foundation incentive structures lack a true upside-sharing mechanism, which can dull appetite for concentrated risk-taking compared to a family office model.
The biggest self-assessed mistakes tend to be errors of omission (passing on OpenAI) rather than active picking errors.
notable: Ryan discloses that MIT's initial decision to back Greenoaks almost didn't happen and was reversed only after Harry's boss slept on it overnight.
